data.title: Macquarie Limited-Term Diversified Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Economic growth in the US slowed further in 1Q25, continuing a trend that began in the second half of 2023. The slowdown became more apparent during the quarter, possibly reflecting consumer and business caution due to rising uncertainty around the Trump administration's policy agenda (both in terms of trade policies and government spending cuts). The US Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained its restrictive monetary policies, which have continued to weigh on growth, although fiscal policy has remained supportive. Consumer spending slowed somewhat, and while this followed robust spending in 4Q24, a cooling labor market could cause consumers to remain cautious in the months ahead. Beyond this, while tariffs are likely to weigh on US growth, a resumption in the Fed's current easing cycle, along with renewed fiscal stimulus, should provide a material positive offset for economic activity in the medium term.

On inflation, the Core Consumer Price Index (Core CPI) again saw a significant increase in January - repeating the start-of-year pattern of recent years - although this was followed by a more subdued reading in February. The Fed's preferred Core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation measure indicated that inflation has been better contained over recent months, although the February reading (in contrast to the CPI) was elevated. The more modest pace of demand, ongoing supply expansion, and a labor market that continues to soften should maintain the broader downward trend in inflation. Even so, the path towards the Fed target looks to be slower and bumpier than was earlier anticipated. Labor demand has continued to cool as hiring trends slow, and labor supply has continued to grow, even as immigration has declined. With the resulting better balance in the labor market, wage growth to continue to ease. While tariffs should boost prices in the coming months, we believe that is likely to have only a one-off impact, especially with most measures of inflation expectations remaining well anchored. Even this one-off impact will likely be tempered by the negative impact tariffs may have on both global and US activity.

Outside the US, euro-zone data continued to point toward subdued economic activity growth in that region, while Chinese growth seemed to benefit somewhat from the stimulus measures announced in 2024. In Europe, the German economic outlook remains concerning in the near term, with structural headwinds buffeting key industries including automobiles and chemicals. Further downside risks stem from the US administration's trade policies. While these all point to downside risks in coming months, we believe a substantially more supportive fiscal outlook will boost growth beyond this with significant government spending on defense and infrastructure now planned over the next few years. Given the degree of slack currently present in the German economy, we do not anticipate materially higher inflation. The European Central Bank (ECB), after cutting rates by 100 basis points in 2024, followed this up with additional 25 basis-point rate cuts in January and March (one basis point is a hundredth of a percentage point). An additional two-and-a-half 25 basis-point cuts over the remainder of 2025 are now priced into rates markets.

The sharp rise in German bond yields that we saw following the recent fiscal-spending announcement serves as a reminder that fiscal outlooks remain a potential cause for concern in many European countries. Europe also remains susceptible to geopolitical events that can affect energy security, with the Russia-Ukraine war continuing to be a potential source of inflationary concern, along with ongoing tension in the Middle East. While the stimulus measures announced by a range of Chinese policymakers over recent months are helping to stabilize activity in the near term, the structural headwinds facing the Chinese economy remain strong, especially the ongoing and deepseated problems in the property sector. In contrast to most other major central banks, economic conditions in Japan, especially wage and inflation outcomes, are pushing the Bank of Japan (BoJ) to further increase rates as it continues to move away from its previous negative-rate policies. After raising rates in January, the rates markets expect a further increase in the months ahead.

While the higher bond yields at the start of 2025 provided attractive entry levels, the subsequent decline in yields since then suggests a more cautious stance is warranted. We will continue to consider increasing duration as yields rise but also reduce exposure when the market looks rich. In addition, geopolitical considerations such as the new US administration's policies are increasingly affecting bond yields, and the response of other countries to these policies will also be important. On credit, while the risk aversion we saw in markets has caused spreads to widen somewhat, this comes from a historically tight starting point. While risks to growth have increased, the prospect of further policy support (both monetary and fiscal) is likely to benefit the medium-term growth outlook. Moreover, while spreads remain relatively tight despite the recent widening, all-in yields remain high relative to recent history and expectations of positive total returns should drive demand.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Limited-Term Diversified Income Fund (MUTF:DTINX) Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Bloomberg 1-3 Year US Government/Credit Index.

Key contributors to performance included:

  • Yield curve management
  • Allocations to commercial mortgage-backed securities and mortgage-backed securities

Key detractors from performance included:

  • An allocation to asset-backed securities (ABS)
  • An allocation to high yield corporates

Outlook

In 1Q25, investors shifted their focus to the potentially deleterious effects of Trump's policies, including tariff wars, federal layoffs, and spending cuts. As a result, markets pulled back from historically rich valuations. Consumer and business sentiment was noticeably weaker, even as hard data remained resilient. Clarity around the scope of potential changes would help assuage market concerns but that may take some time to emerge. Meanwhile, there appears to be some agreement between market participants and the Fed that there will likely be two further cuts this year. That said, the potential for unexpected developments in the economy and the Fed's reaction remains a concern for us.

Our strategy remains a defensive one, targeting attractive spread and yield advantage over the benchmark while maintaining high levels of liquidity. We tactically added some risk in the recent bout of market volatility but remain at the low end of risk historically. We have moved duration from a modest long to closer to home.

data.title: Macquarie Large Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

It's hard to believe the DeepSeek event that caused a stir within the artificial intelligence realm occurred only last quarter. It feels like a lifetime ago. Since then, we experienced an onslaught of news and events that built additional risk into the economic and financial system, causing a shift in sentiment for US equities. The market generated negative returns over what was a volatile quarter and left many question marks in its wake.

Government policy and the opaque threat of tariffs left both corporations and consumers at a standstill. Consumer data showed signs of softening, with areas like consumer spending growth, auto loans, and credit card debt a potential concern. The greatest area of angst emerged from corporations. Many were paralyzed as they awaited what government policy will mean for their businesses. This eroded confidence, causing a slowdown in business spending and lowering earnings expectations for the coming quarters.

Big tech and AI-related stocks were hit hardest. These stocks had been experiencing outsized returns compared with the rest of the market as much of the investment community had been singularly focused on the narrative. The market broadened out as market leadership shifted to companies that had been all but forgotten over the prior 24 months. While masked by severe negative returns in the mega-cap companies, some sectors, such as consumer staples, industrials, and healthcare, generated positive returns during the quarter.

Risk factors played an important role in the market during the quarter but remain elevated. Beta, a measure of market risk, was down significantly. However, within large-cap growth equities, the index exposure to beta was still historically elevated. Another risk factor that did not yet correct was momentum. Given the scale of relative strength many of the high momentum stocks had, it may take time for that risk factor to unwind.

Elsewhere within financial markets, Treasury yields fell, the dollar weakened against a basket of major currencies, and oil was down slightly.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Large Cap Growth Fund (MUTF:IYGIX) Institutional Class shares posted negative absolute returns but outperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Russell Growth Index. While the markets were challenged, the environment favored our style of investing on a relative basis as performance gravitated toward higher-quality businesses.

At a sector level, stock selection in financials, consumer discretionary, and information technology contributed most, along with an overweight allocation to the financials sector. Stock selection in communication services and healthcare detracted the most.

At the individual stock level, the greatest contribution was attributable to not owning Tesla (TSLA), and our positions in Intercontinental Exchange Inc. (ICE) and Visa Inc. (V) Tesla faced well-publicized headwinds last quarter that may bleed into future periods. This has remained a constant stock of debate among the investment community and is volatile as a result. The business has never met our quality standards and we are happy to sit on the sidelines of this battleground stock. ICE is a leading financial exchange operator, fixed income data provider, and mortgage technology company. While many know it as the owner of the New York Stock Exchange, the company is well diversified and essentially a monopoly across several critical businesses like energy futures trading, end-to-end digital mortgage origination, and fixed income pricing needed for exchange-traded funds. The stock recently fluctuated with interest rates but drove strong revenue growth in 2024 and should compound shareholder return over the long term through its diversified set of businesses. Visa, the US-based financial technology company, is consistently one of the most profitable business models we can invest in. The stock fell under pressure in 2024 as there were expectations for guidance revisions, volume growth slowed, and chatter heightened around anti-competitive practices. The core business may be more mature in the US but still grows at a healthy and durable pace. Meanwhile, the cash-to-card conversion is still underpenetrated internationally, and the company's Value Add Services segment is still immature and has a long runway for growth. The recent rerating in the stock was warranted, and we believe long-term trends are still quite favorable.

The largest individual detractors from performance relative to the benchmark were not owning Meta Platforms (META), not owning Eli Lilly & Co. (LLY), and our position in Electronic Arts Inc. (EA) Meta stock was slightly negative to end the quarter but relative to the benchmark and other communication services stock performed well. Having a zero weight relative to the large benchmark allocation hurt. We continue to follow this company closely and while we have become incrementally more constructive, we still have lingering concerns about the business model and worry the cyclical weakness in advertising spend could create further pressure. Eli Lilly stock recovered following a period of volatility in the second half of 2024 as investors digested the possibility of competition, sustained compliance for patients taking its weight-loss therapy, and side effects, among other threats that the valuation hadn't reflected. The stock recovered some value in 1Q25, but we continue to be on the sidelines. Lastly, Electronic Arts is one of the leading video game developers with a portfolio that includes franchises like Madden NFL, EA Sports FC, and The Sims. While the company transitioned to digital, a tailwind for margins, and maintained nearly impenetrable competitive positions across several popular genres, it also made some acquisition missteps into mobile and has been unable to further monetize its competitive strengths. The next catalyst for a stock rerating has been absent, and we became concerned the quality characteristics that made this business so compelling were declining. With that, we exited the position.

During the quarter, we exited two positions, including Electronic Arts, discussed above, and added four stocks to the portfolio. One of our new positions is in IDEXX Laboratories Inc. (IDXX), an animal health company with a primary focus on diagnostics for companion pets. The stock was weak over the past year as the COVID-19 pandemic pet boom and associated veterinary clinic visits normalized. The company is dominant in the space with a large installed base of its diagnostic equipment, which in turn drives the consumables business - a classic razor/razorblade model. As the large population of pets adopted during the pandemic age, there will be an increased need for vet services and testing. We took advantage of weakness in shares to start a position.

Outlook

It seems increasingly clear to us that stock market volatility is likely to persist in the intermediate term, driven by heightened uncertainty around economic growth, business confidence, and consumer health. This mix of limited visibility into key economic indicators creates a challenging investment environment, complicating decisions on risk-taking. Moreover, the market has been progressively extending risk exposure for some time now, providing a vulnerable starting point as conditions become more uncertain. Volatility doesn't necessarily mean a major market drawdown, but we believe forward market returns could be challenged.

These periods are good for stable high-quality growth businesses. Companies in which investors have a high degree of confidence that relatively strong fundamentals can be maintained tend to command a premium for that consistency. This should favor our style of investing.

As mentioned above, underlying risk factors remain elevated and are having an outsized negative impact on stocks that characterize these risks (i.e. momentum and beta). We have been underweight these factors, which was a drag on relative performance in 2023/2024 but should be a tailwind as it reverses.

History has shown that over entire market cycles the market favors companies with strong long-term fundamentals. Lower-quality beta and momentum-driven rallies can boom, but they are subject to volatility and sharp reversals. A quality approach has inherent advantages in the long run, and we continue to maintain that discipline. We believe we are in the early innings of realizing those advantages.

data.title: XLF: Keep Your Finger On The Economy's Pulse With This Sector SPDR
data.description:
Of all the various sector ETFs known as the "Spiders", two of them stand out as the most indicative of a market's salient strengths: technology and finance. That being said, tech may be at the forefront of investor attention now because of AI proliferation, but those who understand that the pulse of the economy lies in the financial sector tend to closely follow The Financial Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLF), which tracks the Financial Select Sector Index, also called the "underlying".

Today, we'll explore this interesting sector fund from an educational standpoint: what it is, what it is intended to do, who is it ideal for, how is it structured, what are the risks, and so on.

About The Financial Select Sector SPDR

This sector is focused on the largest financial entities in the United States, which includes banks and non-banking financial institutions like insurance companies, credit and loan originators, financial advisory and credit risk assessment companies, and so on.

An interesting side note here is that Real Estate Investment Trusts or REITs were also a part of XLF initially, but were compiled under a different ETF in September 2016 because the underlying index was reconstituted to exclude REITs the year before, and that gave rise to an ETF dedicated to real estate entities: The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLRE). XLF shareholders received "an in-kind distribution of the XLRE shares" due to this portfolio shuffle.

The inception date for the XLF was in December 1998, and it is advised by State Street Global Advisors Funds Management Inc. It is managed by Systematic Equity Beta Group and distributed by ALPS Portfolio Solutions Distributor, Inc.

The fund is passively managed, so you're likely to see a tracking error in the range of around 11% to 13% overall. It is also quite volatile, with a standard deviation of around 17, which doesn't compare favorably with the ETF median of around 13. It's also interesting to note that the short interest on XLF gives you an indication of how bullish or bearish investors are on the financial sector. Just something to be aware of.

XLF holds about $49 billion in AUM (assets under management) and comes with a 0.08% expense ratio that's about par for the course for broad index and sector ETFs. The fund has a trailing yield of 1.40%, which is about half the sector median, but has been paying dividends for the past 25 years.

As I said, this is a good way to keep your finger on the pulse of the economy, which is evident from the way the yield dropped during the GFC, gradually exhibiting signs of recovery over the next five years; a brief recessive economy later, yields grew strongly until the pandemic, but have now recovered to pre-GFC levels and showing signs of health. Note how XLF's yield kept growing through the dotcom bubble; since financials were mostly protected from the crashes of private-equity-funded tech companies of the day, they escaped relatively unscathed.

What is XLF Structured to Do?

Now that we know what the fund is, let's look at what it's intended to do. As you can guess, its goal is to track the financial sector of the S&P 500 (SP500) and replicate that sector's total return over various periods of time. Let's see if it's been able to do that. As a comparator, we'll use the underlying index.

Aside from a few variances (which reflect the 11% to 13% tracking error we saw earlier), the fund has tracked the index pretty closely. That doesn't tell us all that much, so let's see how it has performed against the broader S&P 500 that includes all 11 GICS sectors, as well as its (smaller) peer ETF, the Vanguard Financials Index Fund ETF Shares (VFH), and the S&P 500 Financials Index Sector (SP500-40).

Over the past decade, XLF has underperformed all other comparators on a price return basis. What we can reasonably deduce from this is that the ETF is not really structured for outperformance - even against its own benchmark and similar indexes. Logically, that should tell investors to stay away from the fund, but the 10-Year timeframe only tells us so much.

The reason, I believe, is that financial companies sometimes operate in cycles within the broad gamut. For instance, if banks are down as a subgroup of the financial sector, that will reflect on the ETF as well.

Our job as investors, therefore, is to not blindly follow or invest in a sector without fully understanding the subtle forces that alter the fortunes of a particular subsector. A good way to structure your portfolio would be to look for broad sectoral ETFs like XLF, but also explore other options within that universe of stocks - that could be a specific group of tickers or even a more focused ETF such as the Invesco KBW Bank ETF (KBWB), which, as you can see below, did well between the pandemic (ZIRP period) all the way through to the regional banking crisis. So, rotating into this ETF in 2020 and out of it in early 2023 would have helped a core holding of XLF.

Who is the XLF ETF Suitable For?

Now comes the question, who is the ideal investor for this type of sector ETF. Let's break it down:

  1. Investors that are bullish on the broad financial sector
  2. Those that want exposure to the top financial institutions in the U.S., both banking and non-banking
  3. Those looking to hedge sectoral holdings that don't perform well in high-interest rate environments, such as consumer discretionary. See the comparison table below.

Risks to Investing in XLF

The obvious risk for financials is a recessionary economy, when money flows tend to subside and bide their time. During these periods, the large caps in the world of finance tend to perform poorly because they need higher capital reserves and will likely have an unusually high percentage of non-performing assets.

Another risk is high-interest rates, because even though banks tend to do well, other subsector components such as those in consumer finance, as an example, might cut their dividends or stop increasing them. Case in point is American Express (AXP). AXP was paying a quarterly dividend of $0.25 per share in the early '90s, but that was reduced to $0.225 at the end of 1994 and subsequently slashed down to $0.08 per share quarterly payout in 2000.

Although these are the main risks, there are also tail-risk events like the regional banking crisis that could push the ETF down due to the heavy weighting to banks. This last risk brings us to the ETF's composition.

As we can see, any disruption to the three or four major industry holdings could drag down the ETF, and the inverse is true as well: if a particular sub-sector does extremely well for whatever reason, XLF will benefit.

Final Thoughts

The financial sector is one of the most important ones that measures an economy's health at a point in time. When financials do well, most other sectors tend to flourish. Your job as an investor is to determine the right entry point and exit point based on macro developments. However, beware of chasing false trends, because in the case of the regional banking crisis, most of the larger banks suffered from the backlash.

They didn't actually underperform; in fact, the bulk of the big banks continued to post strong earnings growth, but the market underwent a 'fear cycle', decimating billions in market capitalization. If you were a patient investor and refused to sell off on these fears, you'd have experienced a handsome return thereafter.

The XLF, like most other investments, requires doing your due diligence. This ETF can fit into portfolios as a proxy for the broader financial sector. With its low expense ratio, it brings an inexpensive source of diversification or strategic exposure.

This article answers these three main questions about XLF:

  1. How do changes in interest rates impact XLF?
  2. Is XLF diversified within different sub-sectors within the financial sector?
  3. How much of an impact do economic conditions have on XLF?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Enovix Corporation: Great Potential, But Great Risk As Well
data.description:
One rather interesting company for investors to be aware of is Enovix Corporation (NASDAQ:ENVX). With a market capitalization of $1.63 billion, it's not exactly a large firm. However, it operates in a very interesting space and is aiming to achieve significant growth before too long. Management has been making investments aimed at achieving growth, and a partnership that it has with an unnamed player in the cell phone market offers the opportunity for it to achieve remarkable upside.

While this is all positive, there is a downside to the business. Simply put, the firm generates almost no revenue and has seen continued losses and cash outflows. Admittedly, the cash flow situation is gradually improving. But at the end of the day, this makes the firm a bet on it being able to differentiate itself in a rather crowded, but growing, space. For a value investor like me, it's difficult to view such a company in a favorable light. But because of the potential that the business offers, I do believe that assigning it a speculative ‘hold’ rating is appropriate at this time.

An interesting firm

The management team at Enovix Corporation describes the company as a designer, developer, and manufacturer of next-generation lithium-ion battery cells. The company's technology has been pitched by management as a new type of ‘architecture’ that increases the amount of energy density and storage capacity compared to conventional battery cells. Although the company believes that there is potential for it in various industries, including the development of IoT devices, solutions for the defense industry, and even the electric vehicle market, the big emphasis for the firm seems to be right now on all things mobile.

This is a massive industry that continues to expand, though that expansion has been admittedly slow as of late. This year, for instance, it's expected that there will be about 1.26 billion smartphone sales worldwide. That's 2.3% above what the industry saw last year. In certain key markets, such as China, the industry is growing more rapidly. Even though iOS sales are expected to decline by 1.9% in China, Android sales are forecast to decline by 5.6% year over year. In a previous article, written about Apple (AAPL), I talked about subsidies that were being provided by the Chinese government. Although Apple ended up making changes that allowed some of its devices to be eligible for those subsidies, the subsidies themselves were largely targeted toward lower cost devices, which the Android market dominates. While the average selling price of an iPhone is expected to be around $1,048 this year, the average selling price for Android phones is considerably lower at $293.

The company's technology makes possible the use of 100% active silicon and eliminates the need for graphite in the battery’s anode. The advantage here from this innovation is significant because a battery can, in theory, store over twice as much lithium than it can graphite. This ultimately boosts the batteries' energy density and capacity as a whole. You would think that this fact would lead to smartphone manufacturers using nothing but silicon. However, historically, it has swollen and caused cracks in the phones. This has a negative impact on not only performance, but the safety for users as well. Enovix Corporation’s architecture helps to ‘accommodate’ this swelling while also reducing the chance of cracking occurring.

Management has had some interesting interactions with the largest smartphone companies on the planet. In fact, according to the firm, seven of the top eight largest producers have been receiving samples of its batteries. This represents about $7 billion of the $12 billion smartphone battery market. Of course, management is not willing to settle only on this. Although not the primary focus, the firm is also targeting the $8 billion battery industry for IoT devices, the $4 billion computing battery market, and the $3 billion battery category for the defense industry. Management also believes that the cell architecture that the company has developed is ‘well-suited’ for the electric vehicle industry. This is a market that, this year, is estimated to be worth $101 billion globally. And by the year 2040, it's expected to multiply by more than five to $523 billion.

It appears to me as though the reason why Enovix Corporation is prioritizing the smaller smartphone battery industry is because of the needs of increased capacity for smartphones moving forward. AI-enabled apps are already more energy intensive than apps that don't have AI factored into them. And in recent years, smartphone companies have been under pressure to boost capacity in order to meet this challenge. But there have been certain size and technological constraints that have left the industry looking for a solution.

In order to tackle this opportunity, Enovix Corporation has been investing heavily into establishing its manufacturing and research and development footprint. In addition to having its operations based in the US, it also has research and development functions and related activities that it conducts in India. On top of this, it has three factories in South Korea, as well as a significant manufacturing presence in Malaysia.

If management is correct, then this year will see the first launch of smartphones that utilize its technology. If everything goes according to plan, the opportunity to scale in 2026 exists, with the firm hoping to achieve hundreds of millions of dollars of revenue. To put this in perspective, each of its production lines is forecasted to generate $150 million in revenue annually. This would be achieved through the production of 1,650 units each hour and would require only about $60 million in spending per line that it establishes.

Back in 2024, Enovix Corporation struck a deal with an unnamed smartphone producer. The only thing that we know about it is that it is one of the five largest players in terms of market share in China. We don't really know who this other company is. It very well could be Xiaomi Corporation. But at the same time, it could be Huawei Technologies, BBK Electronics (which makes both the OPPO and vivo brands), or Honor Device Co. Although Honor is smaller than Apple in terms of market presence, the other four companies are actually larger. For instance, in the first quarter of 2025, Xiaomi reported 13.3 million units shipped, to snag the top leading market share of 18.6%. That compares to the 13.8% it had just one year earlier. At the same time, Huawei saw its market share climbed from 17% to 18%. The OPPO brand remained flat at 15.7% year over year, while the vivo brand saw its share dip from 14.6% to 14.4%. Unfortunately, Apple reported a decline from 15.6% to 13.7%.

In order to achieve its goal of launching with at least one smartphone model this year, Enovix Corporation has been tasked with achieving certain milestones. During the first quarter of 2025, it achieved the third of these, finalizing the chemistry, obtaining precise smartphone cell dimensions, and moving forward with the development of the final samples to be sent to the customer for the qualification process this summer. What's interesting here is that this agreement is not exclusive. In fact, management has been very open about the fact that it continues to engage with other smartphone producers so that the firm can scale as quickly as possible. At the same time, it continues to focus some on its other markets. As an example, it is currently working with two different smartphone glasses customers, and it has two different OEM partners in the electric vehicle market.

As great as this opportunity is, this remains very much a ‘show me’ story. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the company for the last three fiscal years. And in the chart below, you can see financial results for the first quarter of 2025 compared to the first quarter of 2024. Revenue has risen in recent years, but it is still small for a firm with such a sizable market capitalization. What's more, while losses and cash outflows are improving, it is still hemorrhaging cash. Considering that the business has a net cash position of only $68.6 million today, it might not be long before it has to raise some additional funds.

Normally, I would be rather bearish about a firm like this. But the technology that the company is working on is clearly advantageous and there are multiple large markets it can tackle. The lead smartphone customer agreement that it continues to make progress on is certainly encouraging as well. And another positive is that the business does not need to achieve that much cash flow in order to be fairly valued. In the chart below, you can see how much adjusted operating cash flow it would need in order to trade at 10 times, 20 times, or 30 times, on a price to adjusted operating cash flow basis. That chart also shows how much EBITDA it would need in order to trade those same multiples on an EV to EBITDA basis. Compared to the revenue the business is generating today, this is a herculean hurdle. But because of the push toward commercialization that the company is achieving good progress on, I am more optimistic than I otherwise would be.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I do think that Enovix Corporation is an intriguing prospect. It's honestly not the kind of opportunity for me because I am a value investor. I don't really care for speculation, and I like companies that are cheap and fundamentally sound. Unfortunately, Enovix Corporation doesn't meet any of these qualifications. Having said that, the company is interesting, and I do think that, for those who don't mind taking on risk, it makes for a decent ‘hold’ candidate.

data.title: Meta: Seizing A Golden Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

It is no surprise that such a high-quality wide-moat company like Meta Platforms (META) consistently keeps investors happy as the stock rallied by around 24% since my December 2024 bullish call. Meta remains at the forefront of innovation with ambitious goals in the AI domain. The company's profitability keeps expanding despite the quite aggressive ramp up of R&D spending, which is a massive fundamental green flag. The stock remains undervalued at the moment, meaning that my take is still 'Strong buy' as I continue seizing this golden opportunity by expanding my META position.

Fundamental Analysis

Meta remains a compelling investment opportunity from the fundamental perspective. The company continues successfully balancing robust growth, profitability expansion, and ramping up investments in innovation.

Meta's TTM growth rates across its P&L is absolutely stellar. All key profitability metrics are significantly outpacing revenue growth, which indicates unmatched ability to create shareholders' value from the top-line expansion. Meta's diluted EPS growth rate of almost 50% is absolutely insane for a company with a market cap close to $2 trillion.

Due to its stellar profitability, the company's balance sheet is fortress-like. Capital allocation is exemplary and prudent as the management consistently maintains a quite wide $20+ billion net cash position. The number of outstanding shares is also very stable, which is beneficial for preserving value per share. As a result, Meta's tangible book value per share increased from $50 to $65 year-over-year as of the end of FQ1 2025.

With such a strong financial position and profitability expansion outpacing revenue growth, ramping up innovation spending becomes much easier. Meta's R&D spending has been increasing quite aggressively over the last few years and it reached 20.5% of revenue on a TTM basis as of the end of FQ1. In absolute terms, it totals a staggering $45.8 billion.

The scale of investment reflects Meta's firm conviction in long-term returns from innovation spending, especially AI. To maintain a balance between its R&D spending spree and preserving a healthy financial position, the management is working on introducing new financial considerations. The company is seeking opportunities to optimize its capital structure for flexibility and cost efficiency, which I see from the recent news regarding the company's pursuing to rise $26 billion debt to finance ambitious AI projects. There are additional $3 billion fundraising expected from private investment firms, so the total fundraising endeavor is $29 billion. This capital is earmarked for the construction of next-generation AI data centers across the United States.

It is crucial to explain that Meta's AI strategy spans far beyond just AI infrastructure buildout and internal R&D spending. The company has recently embarked on a high-profile talent acquisition spree, which is evident from the recent hiring of several top OpenAI researchers to bolster its AI efforts. This move is a part of Mark Zuckerberg's broader drive to assemble a world-class superintelligence team. I consider this endeavor quite promising due to Zuckerberg's proven record of building rockstar teams.

Meta's stellar past M&A activities like acquisitions of Instagram and WhatsApp also means that the recent $14.3 billion acquisition of 49% in Scale AI has also solid potential to pay back over the long haul. By the way, Scale's CEO, Alexander Wang, will lead the above-mentioned superintelligence team. Meanwhile, there are also advanced discussions regarding the potential acquisition of PlayAI, a voice-cloning startup. This looks like a sound consideration as the deal can potentially contribute to enriching Meta's smart glasses and AI assistant offerings.

Thus, we see that Meta is firing all cylinders to boost its potential to capitalize on AI for longer. The strategy looks well-rounded as the company balances between internal R&D, CapEx, talent acquisition, and M&A. Therefore, it is reasonable that Wall Street analysts are extremely bullish about Meta's prospects. Among the most fresh reputable Wall Street target price upgrades were made by UBS, boosting from $683 to $812. Analysts of UBS expressed their confidence in the company’s ability to extract incremental revenue from AI products, especially Business Messaging and Meta AI.

Valuation Analysis

Meta looks extremely cheap from the forward P/E ratio perspective. The current P/E ratio of around 30 looks very attractive for a company delivering 15%+ year-over-year revenue growth and aggressive profitability improvements. In addition, the estimated FY2028 P/E ratio is expected to dip below 20 due to the expected EPS growth.

Now, the DCF it is. The financial model's WACC remains 7.7% because the Fed is still quite hawkish. More than 38 Wall Street analysts cover the company's revenue forecast for FY2026-2027, making the sample representative and reliable. Therefore, my DCF's first two years' revenue assumptions rely on consensus. For the period between FY2028 and FY2030, I incorporated a 50-basis point revenue growth rate deceleration per year.

The company's TTM levered FCF margin is 21.5%. We see that Meta's operating leverage remains intact, meaning that implementing a 100 basis points yearly expansion is reasonable. Meta's accelerated AI investments and success of the stock make me upgrade the constant growth to figure out Meta's terminal value ('TV') from 4% to 4.5%.

The fair share price is $842. This indicates a 15% discount compared to the last close, which is a compelling opportunity.

Mitigating Factors

While META is one of the 'Magnificent 7' stocks and is among the top largest companies in the world my market cap, there are notably larger ones like Nvidia, Apple, or Microsoft. These companies have a significant impact on the entire U.S. stock market in both directions. For example, some adverse news about one of these giants might lead to a broader stock market panic, which will likely adversely affect Meta's share price as well. In addition to this risk, CNN suggests that the U.S. equities market is approaching the 'Extreme Greed' area. This means that investors are quite aggressive in their expectations, which also makes the stock market more vulnerable to panic selloffs due to just some noisy headlines.

From the more fundamental perspective, I want to mention competition, concentration, and regulatory risks among the most significant ones. Google remains the undisputed leader in the global digital advertising industry, while Amazon's digital advertising business is also gaining its momentum. Competition is a significant risk, particularly because digital advertising is Meta's only profitable segment that also generates around 99% of the company's sales.

Last but not least, boasting a multi-billion social media audience globally means intense regulatory scrutiny for Meta. Complying with legislation of hundreds of countries is extremely challenging and costly. For example, a very large market like Brazil recently ruled that social media platforms will bear liability for users' posts. This looks like a quite complicated issue for Meta as the move 'paved the way for individuals to be able to sue digital platforms if illegal content that is brought to the companies’ attention is not deleted'.

Conclusion

Meta remains a strong investment opportunity driven by strategic investments in growth and innovation, especially related to the AI field. The company's fortress-type balance sheet positions META well to invest aggressively in protecting its moat.

data.title: Macquarie High Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Macquarie High Income Fund

Market review

US high yield bonds, as measured by the ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index, returned 0.97% for 1Q25, while European and global high yield bonds returned 0.66% and 1.25%, as measured by the ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index and the ICE BofA US Dollar Global High Yield Index, respectively.

The ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index returned 1.39% in January as a positive risk tone across markets drove spreads toward historical lows. The rally was underpinned by a combination of positive macroeconomic data, a solid start to 4Q earnings, and favorable technicals that followed weaker performance in December. The market was also supported by low default activity. CCC-rated bonds were the strongest performers, returning 1.70% for the month, followed by B-rated and BB-rated securities, which returned 1.42% and 1.29%1.29%, respectively.

Volatility picked up meaningfully in February as weaker economic data and tariff uncertainty brought growth concerns to the foreground. Consequently, spreads widened 18 basis points, with notable decompression by rating category (one basis point is a hundredth of a percentage point). Still, total returns remained positive as wider spreads were more than offset by lower Treasury yields. The ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index returned 0.67% for the month with bonds rated BB, B, and CCC returning 0.72%, 0.60% and 0.61%, respectively. Fundamentally, 4Q earnings generally exceeded expectations, though we noted sector-level dispersion and forward guidance from management teams was more mixed.

In March, the risk-off tone worsened as weaker growth data and persistently elevated core inflation readings stoked stagflation concerns while policy uncertainty remained high. The ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index declined 1.07% as spreads widened 67 basis points on average while Treasury yields were volatile, ending the month only modestly lower. Unsurprisingly, rating decompression accelerated during this period with bonds rated CCC, B, and BB declining 2.81%, 1.31%, and 0.55%, respectively, as higher-quality risk meaningfully outperformed.

For the quarter, returns remained positive for bonds rated BB and B, returning 1.43% and 0.77% respectively, while bonds rated CCC lagged and fell 0.89%. The leading sectors were food & beverage, which gained 2.06%; financials, up 1.89%; and cable/satellite, up 1.77%. The weakest sectors were diversified media, which declined 0.90%; paper/packaging, down 0.69%; and automotive, up 0.03%.

New-issue volume totaled $68.3 billion for the quarter, down 22% year over year. Refinancings comprised 75% of this activity, followed by mergers and acquisitions (M&A) at 15.3%, and general corporate purposes at 6.6%. The 12 month par-weighted US high yield default rate ended the quarter at 1.20%, and the outlook for the 2025 default rate stood at approximately 2%−3%.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie High Income Fund (MUTF:IVHIX) Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund’s benchmark, the ICE BofA US High Yield Constrained Index.

Key contributors to performance included:

  • An overweight to and security selection within the media sector
  • Security selection within the technology sector
  • Security selection within the utility sector
  • An overweight to the banking sector
  • An overweight to the insurance sector

Key detractors from performance included:

  • Security selection within the leisure sector
  • Security selection within the basic industry sector
  • Security selection within the telecommunications sector
  • Security selection within the consumer goods sector
  • An underweight to and security selection within the real estate sector

Outlook

Following the recent uptick in volatility, we expect sustained policy and macroeconomic uncertainty to keep spreads somewhat elevated from recent lows. Corporate fundamentals entered this period from a position of relative strength, but forward guidance from our management teams suggests that we should expect more sector level dispersion with cyclical and tariff-impacted credits most exposed at this time. Still, solid corporate balance sheets, low default-rate expectations around 2%−3%, and attractive all-in yields for the asset class at 7.66% should help contain upside risk to spreads outside of a recession scenario. As always, we will continue to rely on our disciplined, research-driven investment process to capitalize on opportunities when valuations diverge from fundamentals.
